answer:
Charles Comte distinguishes **three main genres (or historical periods) of domestic slavery**, each characterized by distinct features in the relations between masters and slaves, their employment, and external influences. These are outlined as follows:

1. **First period (ancient times to the fall of the Roman Empire)**: Slavery was universally admitted across European nations, with no species or racial distinction between masters and slaves (who were of the same stock). Slaves were employed in all sorts of occupations except public functions and military service, and masters held near-absolute power without external limits from other nations, classes, or religions.[^1][^2]

2. **Second period (feudal regime)**: Slavery took on a particular character, with slaves generally attached to agriculture and considered part of the glebe (soil they cultivated), passing with the land rather than being sold individually at markets. This led to less harsh treatment due to limited industry (necessitating on-site consumption of products), weak central authority (allowing desertion to other masters), and lords seeking slave support against rivals. "Servitude of the glebe," still present in parts of Europe, exemplifies this and is less miserable than Roman slavery, as family ties were preserved and slaves retained more of their produce.[^3][^4]

3. **Third period (European colonies in America and elsewhere from the 16th century onward)**: Domestic slavery reemerged with slaves of a different species/race from masters (differing in color, features, religion, and language), confined to islands or continental areas for specialized agricultural labor. Products were destined for export (enabling extreme exploitation, reducing slaves to bare subsistence), but masters' power was checked by metropolitan governments that rejected slavery on their home soil, humanitarian sentiments among non-slaveholders, and Christian sects advocating abolition.[^5][^6]

These distinctions serve to frame the varying effects of slavery across history, rather than a strict chronological history.[^1]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL IV*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/02-nature-des-divers-genres-d-esclavage-domestique/translation/#p-11d79c).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL IV*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/02-nature-des-divers-genres-d-esclavage-domestique/translation/#p-de1963).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL IV*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/02-nature-des-divers-genres-d-esclavage-domestique/translation/#p-702055).

[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL IV*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/02-nature-des-divers-genres-d-esclavage-domestique/translation/#p-129cc3).

[^5]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL IV*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/02-nature-des-divers-genres-d-esclavage-domestique/translation/#p-433149).

[^6]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL IV*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/02-nature-des-divers-genres-d-esclavage-domestique/translation/#p-561e11).
